items.q: What happens when 11111111 + 1 is computed in an 8-bit register?
items.correct: Overflow - the result requires 9 bits but only 8 are available.
items.why: Students miss that registers have fixed width. The mathematical result is 9 bits, but the 8-bit register drops the carry-out, producing 00000000 with an overflow flag.

items.q: What does an INNER JOIN return?
items.correct: Only rows with matching values in both tables.
items.misconception: All rows from both tables combined.
items.random_wrong: Only the inner columns of each table.
items.why: Students confuse INNER (intersection) with FULL OUTER (union). INNER drops unmatched rows from BOTH sides; LEFT keeps all from left; RIGHT keeps all from right; FULL OUTER keeps everything.

items.q: What is the purpose of database normalisation?
items.correct: To reduce redundancy and avoid update anomalies.
items.misconception: To make the database run faster.
items.random_wrong: To make the database look neater.
items.why: Students think normalisation is about performance. In fact, heavy normalisation often slows queries. Its purpose is DATA INTEGRITY - preventing inconsistencies by storing each fact once.

items.q: Which register holds the address of the next instruction?
items.why: Correct answer: PC. “MAR” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which is NOT a stage of the fetch-execute cycle?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Compile. “Fetch” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many stages are there in the fetch-execute cycle?
items.why: The real figure is 3. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
